evidence: A2AGatewayAdapter keeps backward-compatible alias state (`_context_to_room`, `_room_participants`, `_pending_tasks`) and passthrough compatibility methods (`_get_or_create_room`, `_rehydrate`, `_create_task`, `_translate_to_a2a`, `_emit_context_event`) that only forward to collaborators.
Core adapter contract uses `AgentToolsProtocol` everywhere even when adapters need smaller capability slices; tests/fakes mirror the oversized surface by implementing many unrelated methods.
Execution abstraction is split between `Execution` protocol and `ExecutionContext` concrete type, but runtime callback signatures still bind to `ExecutionContext`, limiting substitution.
`pyproject.toml` sets default pytest args to `-m 'not integration'`, so the primary test command excludes integration checks unless explicitly overridden.

`tests/support/integration/config.py` enforces integration mode via `_is_integration_mode` plus marker checks, but this policy is implemented by string/marker conventions spread across files rather than a single contract.

`tests/support/integration/markers.py` defines marker decorators, while `tests/support/integration/config.py` repeats marker names in `_LIVE_INTEGRATION_FIXTURE_MARKERS`; marker-policy drift can silently misclassify tests.

`tests/support/events.py` centralizes event factories with permissive `**kwargs` defaults and fixed timestamp literals, making it easy for tests to pass without exercising real event-shape edge cases.

`README.md` presents broad production-ready framework claims and usage paths, but the visible test-governance surface in `pyproject.toml` + support fixtures is oriented to opt-in integration execution rather than always-on contract verification.

Platform tool behavior is implemented in multiple places: `src/thenvoi/runtime/tools.py` (`execute_tool_call` dispatch map), `src/thenvoi/runtime/tool_bridge.py` (argument normalization + fallback dispatch + error formatting), and framework-specific wrappers in `src/thenvoi/integrations/claude_sdk/tools.py`, `src/thenvoi/integrations/parlant/tools.py`, and `src/thenvoi/integrations/langgraph/langchain_tools.py`.

Two adapter base classes duplicate lifecycle/conversion logic (`src/thenvoi/core/simple_adapter.py` and `src/thenvoi/core/control_plane_adapter.py`) with near-identical `history_converter` handling, `on_started`, `on_cleanup`, and `on_event` flow.

`src/thenvoi/preprocessing/default.py` depends on `src/thenvoi/integrations/base.py` for participant formatting, while that integration utility reaches back into runtime concerns, creating blurred package boundaries.

Runner startup logic is repeated across `docker/codex/runner.py` and `docker/claude_sdk/runner.py` even though `src/thenvoi/testing/runner_core.py` centralizes many lifecycle helpers; config parsing/override policy is split between runner files.

findings.summary: Gateway adapter exposes test-anchored compatibility wrappers/aliases that duplicate collaborator APIs instead of maintaining one stable abstraction boundary.
evidence: `A2AGatewayAdapter.__init__` publishes internal alias fields (`_context_to_room`, `_room_participants`, `_pending_tasks`) from collaborator objects.

Adapter defines wrapper methods (`_get_or_create_room`, `_rehydrate`, `_create_task`, `_translate_to_a2a`, `_emit_context_event`) that only delegate to `GatewaySessionManager`/`GatewayTaskCorrelator`.

Tests call wrapper methods and assert alias internals directly (e.g., `test_adapter.py` checks `_context_to_room`/`_room_participants` and calls `_get_or_create_room`, `_rehydrate`, `_translate_to_a2a`).
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single explicit facade contract for gateway state/operations (or test collaborators directly), then deprecate and remove private compatibility aliases/wrappers in one migration to eliminate duplicate API surfaces.

findings.summary: The adapter tool interface is over-broad, forcing consumers and test doubles to implement capabilities they do not use.
evidence: `AgentToolsProtocol` composes messaging, participant, contact, memory, schema-provider, and dispatch contracts into one required type.
`SimpleAdapter.on_message` requires `AgentToolsProtocol`, propagating the full surface to every adapter subclass regardless of actual needs.

`FakeAgentTools` implements the whole surface (including memory/contact/schema methods) to satisfy typing/test use, while `PydanticAIAdapter` separately defines a narrower `PydanticAIToolsProtocol` indicating real usage is smaller.
findings.suggestion: Make `SimpleAdapter` generic over a tool-capabilities protocol (or split `on_message` contracts per adapter family) so each adapter and test fake binds only to required capabilities.

findings.summary: Bridge injects hidden cross-object mutation through `__setattr__`, creating implicit coupling between orchestrator and collaborators.
evidence: `ThenvoiBridge.__setattr__` intercepts `_link` assignment and mutates collaborator state (`router._link`, `participant_directory.set_link`, `event_dispatcher.set_link`).
Tests repeatedly replace `bridge._link` directly with mocks, relying on this non-obvious side effect path instead of explicit dependency APIs.
findings.suggestion: Replace `__setattr__` hook with an explicit `set_link()`/rebind method on the bridge and collaborator interfaces, and update tests to use the explicit API to remove hidden mutation paths.

findings.summary: Execution is declared as a protocol but key runtime extension points still depend on concrete `ExecutionContext`, reducing abstraction value.
evidence: `execution.py` defines `Execution` protocol, but `ExecutionHandler` is typed as `Callable[[ExecutionContext, PlatformEvent], ...]` rather than `Execution`.
`PlatformRuntime.start` requires `on_execute: Callable[[ExecutionContext, PlatformEvent], Awaitable[None]]`, so callers are coupled to concrete context type.
Runtime tests validate callback plumbing around this concrete shape rather than a protocol contract.
findings.suggestion: Retype callback boundaries to the `Execution` protocol (or a smaller execution-callback protocol) and expose only required context methods via that interface, keeping `ExecutionContext` as an implementation detail.

findings.summary: Bootstrap tests are over-mocked and provide weak confidence for a high-fan-in API path.
evidence: `bootstrap_agent` behavior is asserted by patching both internal steps (`load_runtime_config` and `create_agent_from_runtime`) and checking call wiring, not real config-resolution behavior.

`load_runtime_config` and `create_agent_from_runtime` tests each replace core collaborators, so regressions in integration between URL resolution, credential resolution, and agent creation can pass unit tests.
Packet context flags this file as over-mocked (`15 mocks vs 13 assertions`) while the module is widely reused.
findings.suggestion: Add at least one integration-style contract test for `bootstrap_agent` that uses real `load_runtime_config` logic and controlled environment variables, mocking only external boundary effects (for example `Agent.create`) to assert end-to-end resolved runtime values passed into agent creation.

findings.summary: No integration test verifies router dispatch behavior against session TTL/activity semantics.
evidence: `session.py` defines activity-sensitive eviction (`_evict_expired`) and refresh-on-access behavior in `get_or_create`.
Router flow in `src/thenvoi/integrations/a2a_bridge/router.py` depends on `await self._session_store.get_or_create(room_id)` during each routed message.

Existing tests split concerns (`tests/bridge/test_session.py` for store internals, `tests/bridge/test_router.py` for routing), but there is no test combining a TTL-configured store with router message flow to verify session survival/replacement behavior at module boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Add a cross-module async test that wires `MentionRouter` with `InMemorySessionStore(session_ttl=...)`, simulates activity/expiry transitions, and asserts routing updates session state as expected across consecutive messages.

findings.summary: Tests target deprecated `bridge_core` compatibility imports instead of canonical `a2a_bridge` imports.
evidence: Bridge tests import `InMemorySessionStore`/`SessionData` from `bridge_core.session`, not from `thenvoi.integrations.a2a_bridge.session`.
`thenvoi-bridge/bridge_core/session.py` and `handler.py` are compatibility shims using `make_registered_module_shim`.
Coverage through deprecated aliases can miss canonical import-surface regressions (package export/wiring errors) until runtime migration paths change.
findings.suggestion: Add canonical-path import tests (or migrate existing bridge tests) to assert `thenvoi.integrations.a2a_bridge.session` and `.handler` imports and symbol contracts directly, while keeping a minimal separate test for compatibility shims.

findings.summary: Integration marker policy is duplicated across config and marker helpers, creating governance drift risk for live-fixture gating.
evidence: `pyproject.toml` declares marker names (`requires_api`, `requires_multi_agent`, `requires_user_api`).
`tests/support/integration/markers.py` defines decorators for the same marker set.
`tests/support/integration/config.py` hardcodes required marker names again in `_LIVE_INTEGRATION_FIXTURE_MARKERS` as raw strings.
No single source enforces synchronization of declared markers, decorators, and fixture-policy mapping.
findings.suggestion: Create one canonical marker-policy registry (e.g., a typed mapping module) consumed by both marker decorators and live-fixture enforcement, and add a contract test that validates registry entries match `pyproject.toml` marker declarations.

findings.summary: Integration cleanup swallows broad exceptions, which can mask API regressions and leave state-dependent flakiness undetected.
evidence: `cleanup_contact_state` in `tests/support/integration/config.py` catches `Exception` repeatedly for contact/remove/cancel/reject flows and only logs debug messages.
Most cleanup operations continue after failures, so tests can proceed in partially dirty state.
Marker enforcement ensures test intent (`integration`/credentials) but does not require cleanup success as a quality gate.
findings.suggestion: Narrow exception handling to known transient API errors, aggregate cleanup failures, and fail or xfail when settlement cannot be achieved under strict mode; expose a fixture option to allow best-effort cleanup only when explicitly requested.

findings.summary: Shared event factories favor permissive defaults over explicit contract checks, reducing sensitivity to runtime event-schema drift.
evidence: `tests/support/events.py` builders accept `**kwargs` and populate many defaults (including fixed timestamps), which encourages tests to validate happy-path shapes only.
`pyproject.toml` excludes `tests/**` from pyrefly checks, so type-level drift inside test helpers is not statically gated.
`README.md` advertises multiple production-ready integrations, increasing the need for strict cross-event contract coverage rather than permissive helper defaults.
findings.suggestion: Add explicit schema-contract tests for each event helper (required fields, invalid/unknown fields, versioned payload compatibility) and consider enabling targeted static checks for `tests/support/events.py` to catch helper drift early.

findings.summary: Platform tool contracts are duplicated across runtime and adapter layers, creating drift-prone behavior and inconsistent error semantics.
evidence: `execute_tool_call` in `runtime/tools.py` defines its own name→call mapping and error strings.
`runtime/tool_bridge.py` redefines tool-name normalization/error formatting and also includes a legacy fallback dispatch chain.
Framework integrations still maintain per-framework wrappers around the same tool set (`claude_sdk/tools.py` and `parlant/tools.py`), including additional local parsing/formatting logic.
Tool ordering/constants are repeated (`PLATFORM_*_TOOL_ORDER` and `_LANGGRAPH_*_TOOL_ORDER`) instead of derived from a single registry.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical tool registry object (schema, normalization, dispatch, error mapping, ordering) in runtime; make framework integrations consume that registry through a thin adapter interface and remove per-framework duplicated tool wiring.

findings.summary: Simple and control-plane adapter bases duplicate lifecycle/conversion flow, increasing maintenance divergence risk.
evidence: Both base classes store `history_converter`, `agent_name`, `agent_description` and implement nearly identical `on_started` behavior with converter propagation.
Both implement `on_event` with the same conversion pattern (`inp.history.convert(...)` fallback to raw) then call `on_message`.
Both expose identical no-op `on_cleanup` hook with separate implementations.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared adapter lifecycle into one reusable mixin/base (or unify base classes with a typed tool-capability mode flag) so conversion/startup/cleanup semantics are implemented once and specialized only where behavior truly differs.

findings.summary: Preprocessing depends on an integrations utility for core runtime participant formatting, weakening architectural boundaries.
evidence: `DefaultPreprocessor` imports `check_and_format_participants` from `integrations/base.py` even though preprocessing is part of runtime message pipeline.
`integrations/base.py` utility requires `ExecutionContext` semantics and runtime formatter behavior, so integration layer acts as a generic-runtime utility bucket.
This makes dependency direction ambiguous: runtime/preprocessing relies on integration package for behavior that is not integration-specific.
findings.suggestion: Move participant change formatting helper into a runtime/preprocessing module (or dedicated runtime utilities package) and keep `integrations/base.py` scoped to true integration translation concerns.

findings.summary: Docker runner bootstrap policy is split across runner scripts, causing repeated orchestration and inconsistent configuration conventions.
evidence: Both runner scripts repeat startup orchestration steps (read env/config, initialize repo, build prompt, create adapter, create agent, run lifecycle) with runner-specific parsing in file-local helpers.
`runner_core.py` already centralizes large parts of this lifecycle, but policy details (env key precedence, adapter config parsing) remain spread across runner files.

Codex runner introduces several parser helpers (`_parse_transport`, `_parse_approval_mode`, `_parse_reasoning_effort`) while Claude runner uses direct env reads, leading to non-uniform validation patterns.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared typed runner-config composition layer (per-adapter schema + common bootstrap template) so each runner only declares adapter-specific fields while startup flow and validation conventions stay centralized.

findings.summary: Adapter tests reimplement tool dispatch logic inline, coupling tests to duplicated behavior instead of one shared contract helper.
evidence: `tests/adapters/test_crewai_adapter.py` defines `_execute_tool_call` with manual tool-name branching mirroring production dispatch behavior.
`tests/adapters/test_codex_adapter.py` defines custom fake tool schemas and behavior-specific fakes, bypassing shared runtime tool bridge contracts.
When production tool mappings/error contracts evolve, duplicated test-side dispatch can silently diverge from runtime semantics.
findings.suggestion: Provide a shared testing utility that uses the same canonical tool registry/dispatch path as production, and update adapter tests to use it instead of local if/elif dispatch replicas.
